perturbation.original_text: 5.2 If the disclosing Party so requests in writing, the r eceiving Party shall immediately: - 5.2.1 return all Confidential Information including copies to the disclosing Party ; and 5.2.2 destroy or permanently erase all copies made by the receiving Party and procure that anyone to whom the receiving Party ha s supplied copies destroys or permanently erases such copies and any further copies made by them.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 If the disclosing Party so requests in writing, the receiving Party shall, within 90 days: - 5.2.1 return all Confidential Information including copies to the disclosing Party; and 5.2.2 destroy or permanently erase all copies made by the receiving Party and procure that anyone to whom the receiving Party has supplied copies destroys or permanently erases such copies and any further copies made by them.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within 90 days' for returning confidential information and destroying copies introduces a delay that could conflict with legal requirements for data protection and trade secret laws which often necessitate prompt action upon request.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) - Article 17: Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of GDPR grants data subjects the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning them without undue delay. The modified text, allowing for a 90-day delay, potentially conflicts with the 'without undue delay' requirement if the confidential information contains personal data of EU citizens.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day delay for erasing personal data, which contradicts Article 17 of the GDPR, as explained in the Law Explanation. This article mandates erasure 'without undue delay.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes this requirement: 'The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay...'. The 90-day delay directly conflicts with the 'without undue delay' requirement, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.3 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the Law s of England and Wales . Each Party irrevocably agrees to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the C ourts of England to settle any dispute that aris es out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.3 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of England and Wales. Each Party irrevocably agrees to submit to the jurisdiction of any court of their choosing to settle any dispute that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the exclusive jurisdiction to any court of their choosing may contradict established principles regarding choice of law and forum selection clauses. It creates uncertainty about where disputes will be resolved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Brussels I Regulation (Recast) - Regulation (EU) No 1215/2012
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 25 of the Brussels I Regulation (Recast) sets out specific requirements for jurisdiction agreements within the EU, to which the UK was a party during the drafting of the contract. Allowing each party to choose any court undermines the predictability and enforceability requirements of valid jurisdiction clauses under EU law that was in place during the contract creation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original agreement by allowing each party to choose any court, undermining the enforceability requirements. The original text specifies exclusive jurisdiction in the Courts of England, providing predictability. By changing this to 'any court of their choosing,' the modified text introduces uncertainty and potential conflicts of jurisdiction. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation support that this change is problematic because it goes against the spirit of agreements to ensure predictability and enforceability requirements of valid jurisdiction clauses under EU law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 also refers to Regulation (EU) No 1215/2012, indicating that there are rules regarding jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Each Party shall indemnify and hol d harmless the other Party against all loss, damage and expense (including legal expenses) whatsoever and howsoever arising out of or in connection with any breach by the first Party or its employees of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Each Party shall indemnify and hold harmless the other Party against all loss, damage and expense (excluding legal expenses) whatsoever and howsoever arising out of or in connection with any breach by the first Party or its employees of this Agreement, up to a maximum of £100.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the indemnification to exclude legal expenses and capping the amount at £100 could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, especially if a breach causes significant damages. It may violate the principle of providing adequate compensation for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) makes it unlawful for a business to exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract, unless the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness. Limiting the indemnity to £100 and excluding legal expenses is highly likely to be considered unreasonable if it prevents fair compensation for a significant breach, potentially violating UCTA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a limit of £100 and excludes legal expenses from the indemnification clause. This directly contradicts the principle of providing adequate compensation for a breach of contract. The Law Explanation states that under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA), it is unlawful to exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract unless the term is reasonable. Snippet 1, from the Uniform Commercial Code § 2-302, addresses unconscionable contract clauses, stating that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or a clause if it finds it unconscionable. Limiting the indemnity to £100 and excluding legal expenses for a potentially significant breach is highly likely to be considered unreasonable and thus unconscionable, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 The receiving Party shall notify anyone to whom it is authorised to disclose the Confidential Information that it is confidential and procure that they comply with this Agreement. On the request of the d isclosing Party, the r eceiving Party shall procure that they enter into a confidentiality agreement on terms and conditions no less onerous than those contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 The receiving Party shall notify anyone to whom it is authorized to disclose the Confidential Information that it is confidential and encourage them to comply with this Agreement. On the request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party will attempt to procure that they enter into a confidentiality agreement on terms and conditions no less onerous than those contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the obligation from 'procure that they comply' to 'encourage them to comply' and 'procure' to 'attempt to procure' reduces the level of responsibility on the receiving party to ensure compliance by third parties. This might conflict with data protection laws requiring data controllers to ensure that processors implement appropriate technical and organizational measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 28(3) of the UK GDPR requires data controllers to use processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to meet the requirements of the GDPR. Diluting the obligation to ensure third parties comply with confidentiality weakens the controller's (receiving party's) ability to guarantee that processors handle personal data in accordance with data protection laws, potentially violating Article 28(3).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 28 GDPR – Processor - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that processing will meet the requirements of this Regulation and ensure the protection of the rights of the data subject. 1 The processor shall not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. 2 In the case of general written authorisation, the processor shall inform the controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of other processors, thereby giving the controller the opportunity to object to such changes. 1 Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller. 2 That contract or other legal act shall stipulate, in particular, that the processor: processes the personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, including with regard to transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the processor is subject; in such a case, the processor shall inform the controller of that legal requirement before processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest; ensures that persons authorised to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality; takes all measures required p
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the receiving party's obligation to ensure third-party compliance with confidentiality, potentially violating Article 28(3) of the UK GDPR. The original text states the receiving party 'shall procure that they comply' and 'shall procure that they enter into a confidentiality agreement', whereas the changed text weakens this obligation to 'encourage them to comply' and 'attempt to procure'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Art. 28 GDPR, states that 'the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures'. By weakening the obligation to ensure compliance, the modified text diminishes the controller's ability to provide 'sufficient guarantees', thus contradicting the requirement of Article 28(3).
